evidence: Contract metadata is authored separately per contract constant set in both files (e.g., multiple `*IntroducedIn` constants), creating many distributed boundary-version touchpoints for one protocol surface.

`startram_contracts.go` defines `APIConnectionErrorMessage` but the catalog entry hardcodes a second literal (`"Unable to connect to API server"`) instead of using the constant, introducing parallel truth sources for the exported contract message.

Contract identity naming is inconsistent across files and fields: ID/action uses `C2CConnectAction` while descriptor `Name` is `C2CActionConnect`, which weakens boundary predictability for cross-module consumers.

Multiple modules define very large callback-bag runtimes whose defaults mostly bind package-level functions and whose methods just nil-check then forward (e.g. goseg/importer/importer.go importerRuntime, goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go Runtime*Ops families, goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go workflowRuntime).

Startup and main bootstrap layers add additional runtime shells and wrapper constructors over direct calls (goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go startupInitRuntime/startBackgroundServicesRuntime, goseg/main.go bootstrapRuntime/c2cRuntime variants), increasing indirection depth across startup paths.

Config patching has parallel abstraction paths that encode the same field updates twice: typed patch methods (applyConnectivityUpdates/applyRuntimeUpdates/etc.) and confPatchRegistry apply closures (goseg/config/config_update_schema.go), creating duplicate mapping logic over the same SysConfig model (goseg/structs/configs.go).

Event publishing uses a package-global broker plus one concrete implementation, while package-level publish helpers and context wrappers add little behavior beyond forwarding (goseg/docker/events/events.go), and call sites consume these wrappers rather than an explicit dependency (e.g. goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go, goseg/rectify/rectify.go).

goseg/config/config_update_schema.go centralizes parsing, update detection, and mutation in a 1200+ line registry with repeated parse/has/apply closures per field, plus separate has/apply methods for each patch group.

goseg/docker/orchestration/orchestration.go repeats the same public wrapper pattern (validate -> singleton lookup -> method call) across many operations, while CreateVolume/DeleteVolume bypass the runtime object and instantiate network runtime directly.
goseg/system/wifi.go and goseg/logger/logger.go both combine mutable process-wide state, lifecycle control, and low-level side effects in single files, increasing coupling between unrelated concerns.

findings.summary: Contract versioning is fragmented across per-entry constants, increasing boundary drift risk.
evidence: `protocol_contracts.go` defines separate `*IntroducedIn` constants for each action and embeds them directly in catalog entries.
`startram_contracts.go` repeats the same pattern with its own `APIConnectionErrorIntroducedIn`, so version policy for one protocol surface is split across files.
findings.suggestion: Centralize contract lifecycle metadata behind a single registry or shared version policy helper in the contracts package, then reference that source from per-domain entry builders to avoid cross-file drift.

findings.summary: StarTram error contract keeps duplicate message literals instead of one boundary source.
evidence: `startram_contracts.go` declares `APIConnectionErrorMessage` but does not use it in `ContractDescriptor.Message`.
The descriptor uses a hardcoded duplicate string, allowing exported message behavior to diverge from the declared constant without compile-time signal.
findings.suggestion: Use `APIConnectionErrorMessage` directly in the descriptor and apply the same single-source convention to message-bearing contracts package-wide.

findings.summary: Contract identity labels are not canonically aligned across ID/action/name fields.
evidence: In `protocol_contracts.go`, the C2C entry uses `C2CConnectAction`/`ActionC2CConnect` but descriptor name `C2CActionConnect` (order differs).
`startram_contracts.go` uses consistent `APIConnectionError` naming, highlighting inconsistent identity conventions inside the same contracts subsystem.
findings.suggestion: Define and enforce a canonical naming rule for contract ID/token/descriptor fields (including ordering), then normalize existing entries and add a small test that validates naming coherence across catalog entries.

findings.summary: Callback-bag runtimes are over-expanded into pass-through seam layers that add indirection without clear domain boundaries.
evidence: importerRuntime contains many function fields and paired wrapper methods (swapSettings, startramSettings, updateContainerState, waitForBootCode, etc.) that mostly nil-check then forward.

Runtime ops in orchestration split into many tiny Runtime*Ops structs and default builders that primarily bind existing package functions, creating broad seam surfaces with low behavioral encapsulation.
main/startuporchestrator introduce additional runtime wrapper structs and default constructors around already abstracted operations, stacking wrapper-on-wrapper indirection in bootstrap paths.
findings.suggestion: Collapse callback bags into smaller domain ports (e.g. ConfigStore, ContainerControl, TransitionPublisher) with cohesive methods; inject only the ports each workflow uses and remove pure forwarding helper methods that do not enforce policy.

findings.summary: Config mutation is modeled twice (typed patch methods and registry-driven apply closures), creating competing abstractions for the same updates.
evidence: config_update_schema defines applyConnectivityUpdates/applyRuntimeUpdates/etc. on typed patch structs and also defines confPatchRegistry entries with per-key applyUpdates closures writing the same SysConfig fields.
Both paths encode field-level mapping and update semantics, increasing the risk of drift when fields are added/changed in SysConfig.
findings.suggestion: Keep a single authoritative patch abstraction: either consolidate around typed patch apply methods with generated key mapping metadata, or make the registry the single source and delete redundant typed apply logic.

findings.summary: The event broker interface currently wraps a single in-memory implementation plus global accessors, reducing interface honesty and explicit dependency flow.
evidence: events.go defines EventBroker but only transitionBus is provided here; package-level globals (defaultBroker, SetEventBroker/getEventBroker) are used as indirection rather than injecting broker instances into consumers.
Publish* and Publish*WithContext pairs mainly forward through getEventBroker/publishWithFallback, where publishWithFallback only normalizes nil context.
Consumers such as workflow runtime emitters and rectify handlers depend on package wrappers instead of a passed broker contract.
findings.suggestion: Inject a broker dependency into runtimes that publish/consume transitions and narrow the interface to required operations per consumer; remove redundant global publish wrappers that only forward.

findings.summary: Repeated no-context/with-context wrapper pairs create broad wrapper chains with minimal behavioral differentiation.
evidence: subsystem_events exposes StartDockerHealthLoops -> StartDockerHealthLoopsWithContext and StartDockerSubsystem -> StartDockerSubsystemWithContext wrappers that mostly substitute context.Background.
rectify adds runTransitionEventLoopWithoutContext around runTransitionEventLoop with the same pattern.
events has PublishX and PublishXWithContext pairs with similarly thin forwarding.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on context-first APIs as the primary abstraction and keep only high-value convenience wrappers at module boundaries where they add policy (timeouts/retries/logging), not simple forwarding.

findings.summary: Governance docs point to legacy contract files while active contract descriptors live in sibling files
evidence: README.md contract guidance says to define compatibility descriptors in `goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts.go` and startram masking in `goseg/startram/errors.go`.
Active descriptor entries with `ContractMetadata` are defined in `protocol_contracts.go` and `startram_contracts.go`, including IDs, versions, compatibility, and message shape.
This creates a split source of truth where contributors following README can miss the actual files that now carry contract boundary behavior.
findings.suggestion: Update README governance text to name the full contract catalog surface (`contracts.go` registry plus per-domain descriptor files like `protocol_contracts.go` and `startram_contracts.go`) and define a single canonical rule for where new descriptors must be added.

findings.summary: Declared CI contract gate scope omits files that now hold contract compatibility metadata
evidence: README.md CI section documents Runtime Contract Gate path checks for a fixed set of files, including `goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts.go` but not `protocol_contracts.go` or `startram_contracts.go`.
Both omitted files contain contract IDs and compatibility/version metadata (`IntroducedIn`, `Compatibility`, and message contract fields), which are part of external contract behavior.
A contributor can change compatibility descriptors in these files without triggering the documented path-aware quality gate.
findings.suggestion: Expand the contract gate file matrix to include `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go` and `goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go` (or switch to directory-level matching for `goseg/protocol/contracts/*.go`) so governance checks follow the real contract boundary.

findings.summary: Large callback-bag runtimes mix unrelated responsibilities and create structurally brittle entry points.
evidence: collectorRuntime in collectors.go aggregates many unrelated concerns (urbit config, container stats, startram services, backup timing/root, swap/penpai settings) and Construct* functions depend on this single bag.

NetdataRuntime in container/netdata.go carries file IO, volume ops, image selection, container lifecycle, and timer/status polling in one struct, crossing persistence, orchestration, and runtime observation concerns.

RectifyRuntime in rectify.go composes config/container/urbit/persistence seams but still exposes a broad merged runtime shape; this same pattern recurs with different field sets rather than a stable domain abstraction.
findings.suggestion: Define smaller domain-focused interfaces (for example: `ConfigSnapshotProvider`, `ContainerLifecycle`, `BackupMetadataProvider`) and compose them at the call site; remove monolithic callback structs and keep each workflow wired to only the interfaces it actually uses.

findings.summary: Package-level orchestration APIs duplicate method wrappers and validation paths, producing noisy and inconsistent control flow.
evidence: orchestration.go defines many pairs of functions with the same behavior (package function + receiver method), where package functions mostly validate then forward to singleton runtime methods.

CreateVolume/DeleteVolume in orchestration.go do not use the runtime's lifecycle dependency and instead instantiate `network.NewNetworkRuntime()` in both package and method layers, diverging from other operations.
runtime_core.go already provides structured runtime dependency construction, but orchestration.go keeps separate ad-hoc forwarding patterns, indicating competing structural styles.
findings.suggestion: Collapse duplicate forwarding layers by choosing one public surface (runtime instance methods or package facade) and generating/shared helpers for validation + dispatch; route volume operations through the same runtime dependency path as other container operations.

findings.summary: Several modules combine global mutable state, lifecycle orchestration, and execution side effects in single units.
evidence: wifi.go maintains global state (`WifiInfo`, `Device`, `ConfChannel`, loop cancel func, c2c mode) while also implementing command execution, client IO, and long-running loop lifecycle.
logger.go holds global init state (`LogPath`, init lifecycle flags, global sink) while also handling banner rendering, filesystem fallback policy, and writer implementations.

session.go provides detailed in-memory store mechanics and a runtime wrapper API in the same file, while auth.go acts as a pass-through boundary into session/authsession/tokens; state and orchestration responsibilities are not clearly separated.
findings.suggestion: Split each module into explicit layers: pure state store, lifecycle coordinator, and IO/adapter layer; inject stores into orchestrators rather than reading/writing package globals directly.

findings.summary: Test suites repeatedly re-implement runtime seam reset/build helpers instead of sharing a coherent fixture pattern.
evidence: exporter_test.go, disk_test.go, and logger_test.go each define bespoke reset helpers that manually restore many package-level seams/global variables.
importer_test.go and startup_orchestrator_test.go define additional custom runtime builder/reset patterns and string-switched callback override wiring.
The repeated fixture style indicates no shared testing abstraction for seam-heavy modules, increasing divergence and maintenance overhead when runtime seams change.
findings.suggestion: Introduce shared test fixture builders per subsystem (or a common seam-reset helper package) with typed option overrides; replace repeated manual global restoration blocks and string-dispatch override helpers.
